From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

American 1940's Negro league baseball team

| Seattle Steelheads | |
| --- | --- |
| Information | |
| League | * [West Coast Negro Baseball League](/wiki/West_Coast_Negro_Baseball_League "West Coast Negro Baseball League") |
| Location | [Seattle](/wiki/Seattle "Seattle") |
| [Ballpark](/wiki/Ballpark "Ballpark") | * [Sick's Stadium](/wiki/Sick%27s_Stadium "Sick's Stadium") |
| Established | [1944](/wiki/1944_in_baseball "1944 in baseball") |
| Disbanded | [1946](/wiki/1946_in_baseball "1946 in baseball") |
| Nicknames | * Harlem Globetrotters * Seattle Steelheads * Cincinnati Crescents |

The **Seattle Steelheads** were a [Negro league baseball](/wiki/Negro_league_baseball "Negro league baseball") team from [Seattle, Washington](/wiki/Seattle,_Washington "Seattle, Washington"). Owned by Abe Saperstein, they were also known as the Harlem Globetrotters and Cincinnati Crescents, though occasionally the teams split and played each other.

## Founding

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Seattle_Steelheads&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Founding")]

[Abe Saperstein](/wiki/Abe_Saperstein "Abe Saperstein") founded the **Harlem Globetrotters** baseball team in 1944 to complement his world-famous [basketball team](/wiki/Harlem_Globetrotters "Harlem Globetrotters") of the same name.[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1)

Also owned by Saperstein,[[2]](#cite_note-2) the **Cincinnati Crescents** were an All-Star [barnstorming](/wiki/Barnstorm_(sports) "Barnstorm (sports)") baseball team that played in the mid-1940s. The team was managed by [Winfield Welch](/wiki/Winfield_Welch "Winfield Welch"), and featured players such as [Bill Blair](/wiki/Bill_Blair_(1940s_pitcher) "Bill Blair (1940s pitcher)"), [Sherwood Brewer](/wiki/Sherwood_Brewer "Sherwood Brewer"), [Luke Easter](/wiki/Luke_Easter_(baseball) "Luke Easter (baseball)"), [Alvin Gipson](/wiki/Alvin_Gipson "Alvin Gipson"), [Bill Jefferson](/wiki/Bill_Jefferson_(baseball) "Bill Jefferson (baseball)"), [Leaman Johnson](/wiki/Leaman_Johnson "Leaman Johnson"), and [Johnny Markham](/wiki/Johnny_Markham "Johnny Markham"). The Globetrotters and Crescents combined operations and were charter members of the [West Coast Negro Baseball League](/wiki/West_Coast_Negro_Baseball_League "West Coast Negro Baseball League"), changing their name to the **Seattle Steelheads**.[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1)

The Steelheads played in the [West Coast Negro Baseball League](/wiki/West_Coast_Negro_Baseball_League "West Coast Negro Baseball League") and played their first game on June 1, 1946, against the [San Diego Tigers](/wiki/San_Diego_Tigers "San Diego Tigers"), in front of 2,500 fans at Sick's Stadium. Its players included [Cannonball Berry](/wiki/Cannonball_Berry "Cannonball Berry"), [Nap Gulley](/wiki/Nap_Gulley "Nap Gulley"), [Zell Miles](/wiki/Zell_Miles "Zell Miles"), [Rogers Pierre](/wiki/Rogers_Pierre "Rogers Pierre"), [Herb Simpson](/wiki/Herb_Simpson "Herb Simpson"), and [Fay Washington](/wiki/Fay_Washington "Fay Washington"). The league folded after a month of play.

## Controversy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Seattle_Steelheads&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Controversy")]

Catcher and manager Paul Hardy joined the Steelheads while still under contract with the [Chicago American Giants](/wiki/Chicago_American_Giants "Chicago American Giants"), causing the Negro American League to ban its teams from playing games in Seattle.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

## Home fields

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Seattle_Steelheads&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Home fields")]

Their primary home ballpark was [Sick's Stadium](/wiki/Sick%27s_Stadium "Sick's Stadium"). They also planned home games in [Tacoma](/wiki/Tacoma,_Washington "Tacoma, Washington"), [Bremerton](/wiki/Bremerton,_Washington "Bremerton, Washington"), [Spokane](/wiki/Spokane,_Washington "Spokane, Washington"), and [Bellingham](/wiki/Bellingham,_Washington "Bellingham, Washington").[[4]](#cite_note-4)

## MLB throwback jerseys

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Seattle_Steelheads&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: MLB throwback jerseys")]

The [Seattle Mariners](/wiki/Seattle_Mariners "Seattle Mariners") honored the Steelheads when they wore 1946 Steelheads uniforms on [September 9, 1995](/wiki/1995_Seattle_Mariners_season "1995 Seattle Mariners season"), at home against the [Kansas City Royals](/wiki/Kansas_City_Royals "Kansas City Royals"). The Royals wore [Kansas City Monarchs](/wiki/Kansas_City_Monarchs "Kansas City Monarchs") uniforms.[[5]](#cite_note-5) The Mariners beat the Royals 6–4 in front of 39,157 fans at the [Kingdome](/wiki/Kingdome "Kingdome").[[6]](#cite_note-6) The game was attended by former Steelhead player Sherwood Brewer.[[7]](#cite_note-7) The Mariners wore a different variety of the Steelheads uniform on May 16, 2015, on "Turn Back the Clock Night" against the [Boston Red Sox](/wiki/Boston_Red_Sox "Boston Red Sox") at [Safeco Field](/wiki/Safeco_Field "Safeco Field"),[[8]](#cite_note-8) and lost to the Red Sox 4–2. On [June 19th, 2021](/wiki/2021_Seattle_Mariners_season "2021 Seattle Mariners season") and [June 19, 2022](/wiki/2022_Seattle_Mariners_season "2022 Seattle Mariners season") the Mariners again wore the 1946 uniforms as part of [Juneteenth](/wiki/Juneteenth "Juneteenth"). They also wore them on [June 17, 2023](/wiki/2023_Seattle_Mariners_season "2023 Seattle Mariners season") due to not having a game on Juneteenth.[[9]](#cite_note-9) On January 31, 2026, the Mariners announced that the Steelhead uniforms would be worn for every Sunday home game in the 2026 season. [[10]](#cite_note-10)
